stories.headline: Colorado launches wildfire and drought task force as state faces record-dry conditions
stories.summary:
Colorado will convene a multiagency task force in the coming weeks to prepare for what experts broadly expect to be a summer of severe drought and elevated wildfire risk. The task force will draw from agencies focused on water, agriculture and emergency management, and will have authority to fund emergency responses and form ad hoc groups to assist affected communities. The last time such a body was activated was during the 2020-21 drought.

State climatologist Russ Schumacher said the first four months of the current water year are the warmest on record in 131 years of data, and called the margin by which the record is being broken unprecedented. Statewide streamflow forecasts indicate Colorado may receive only 63 percent of normal water flows this year, and statewide snowpack stood at 63 percent of normal as of late February — tying a record low for that date set in 2002.

Cities including Westminster are already developing coordinated public messaging around potential watering restrictions, with water managers warning that back-to-back dry years pose a greater long-term threat than any single drought season. Officials said the goal is to align agencies early so that responses to water shortages and wildfire emergencies are consistent and swift across jurisdictions.

stories.headline: Grass fire injures five, prompts evacuations and highway closures in Thornton
stories.summary:
A rapidly spreading vegetation fire broke out near 84th Avenue and Huron Street in Thornton on February 25, burning approximately 10 acres before firefighters achieved full containment at 2:07 p.m. The blaze erupted during Red Flag conditions, with strong winds driving it quickly eastward through a greenbelt between a residential neighborhood and a commercial zone. Law enforcement conducted door-to-door evacuations, closed several roads including a stretch of Interstate 25, and fully evacuated a nearby high school as a precaution.

Five people sustained non-critical injuries in the incident — four firefighters and one civilian. Fire Chief Stephen Kelley said no residential structures were lost, though some businesses along the fire's southern edge sustained damage, and crews remained on scene overnight to monitor for re-ignition.

Eight fire agencies responded, including departments from Thornton, Westminster, Adams County, Northglenn, Federal Heights and South Adams. The cause of the fire remains under investigation. Kelley described the event as 'a dynamic, rapidly spreading event that challenged many of the departments in the north area.'

stories.headline: Douglas County wildfire burns more than 1,000 acres south of Franktown, forces evacuations
stories.summary:
A wildfire scorched 1,081 acres of dry, grassy terrain south of Franktown on Tuesday, prompting mandatory evacuations of nearby homes and the early release of students from Cherry Valley Elementary School. The Dahlberg fire was first reported at 12:39 p.m. near Dahlberg and Lake Gulch roads in Douglas County, about eight miles south of Franktown and eight miles east of Larkspur. Tinder-dry fuels, drought conditions and winds up to 20 mph allowed the fire to spread rapidly before firefighters achieved full containment just before 5 p.m.

Douglas County sheriff's deputies contacted 20 homes in the fire's path, with 17 choosing to evacuate. An air tanker from Colorado Springs and a helicopter from Broomfield joined metro Denver fire agencies in the response. No building or property damage was reported, and the cause of the fire remains under investigation.

High winds also disrupted operations at Denver International Airport on Tuesday, with the FAA ordering a ground stop that led to the delay of 385 flights and cancellation of 25 others by early evening. A red flag warning was in effect across most of the Front Range and Eastern Plains on Wednesday, with forecasters warning of sustained winds up to 35 mph and gusts reaching 50 mph.

stories.headline: Gov. Polis moves to permanently block release of immigrant employment records sought by ICE
stories.summary:
Gov. Jared Polis filed a motion Friday asking a Denver judge to issue a permanent injunction blocking the release of employment records that U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement subpoenaed roughly a year ago. The records pertain to sponsors of unaccompanied immigrant children who lack legal status and were compiled by the Colorado Department of Labor and Employment. Polis' attorneys argued that ICE's failure to intervene in the ongoing lawsuit or take further enforcement action suggests the records are no longer needed.

The lawsuit was originally filed last June by Scott Moss, a former state labor department employee, who sought to block Polis from complying with the administrative subpoena. The suit has temporarily halted the records' release while litigation proceeded. The Colorado state employees union Colorado WINS, as well as the nonprofit law firm Towards Justice, joined the suit in opposition to compliance.

Moss' attorney Laura Wolf said the legal team was 'glad that, after nearly ten months of litigation, Governor Polis has finally conceded that there is no basis to respond to ICE's immigration enforcement subpoena.' The state labor department has since updated its policy to require a court order before responding to such subpoenas. Moss' attorneys said they had not been given sufficient time to review the settlement offer before it was presented to the court.

stories.headline: Denver mayor says police will detain ICE officers who use excessive force
stories.summary:
Denver Mayor Mike Johnston announced Thursday that Denver Police officers will intervene to detain any ICE agent observed using excessive force against a Denver resident during immigration enforcement operations. Johnston said the city holds its own officers to a use-of-force standard and intends to apply the same benchmark to federal agents operating within city limits. He also stated that no federal officer will be permitted to obstruct city emergency responders from providing lifesaving medical aid.

'If we see any ICE officer using excessive force against the Denver resident, we will step in to detain that officer and remove them from the situation,' Johnston said. He added that the city would investigate and prosecute any ICE agent who assaults, shoots or kills a civilian in Denver, regardless of federal action.

Johnston framed the policy as protective rather than confrontational, saying the city's goal is 'not to provoke, but to protect.' The announcement comes amid ongoing tensions between Denver and the Trump administration over immigration enforcement in sanctuary cities.

stories.headline: Colorado lawmakers split sharply along party lines over Trump's State of the Union address
stories.summary:
Colorado's congressional delegation reacted predictably along partisan lines to President Donald Trump's State of the Union address Tuesday, with the state's four Republican House members applauding the speech and its six Democrats sharply criticizing it. Republicans including U.S. Reps. Gabe Evans, Jeff Crank and Lauren Boebert praised what they described as accomplishments on border security, energy production and economic growth. Rep. Jeff Hurd, who had lost Trump's endorsement over a tariff dispute, called the address a chance to reflect on progress.

Democrats were uniformly critical. U.S. Sen. John Hickenlooper predicted before the speech that Trump would ask Americans 'not to believe what we can see with our own eyes' and denounced the administration's immigration enforcement as 'lawless.' Rep. Jason Crow of Aurora, one of several Democrats the Justice Department unsuccessfully sought to indict over a video advising troops to refuse illegal orders, attended the address and said of Trump's failed effort: 'He tried to imprison us. But he failed.'

Rep. Diana DeGette of Denver was the sole Colorado delegation member who declined to attend, stating she would not 'legitimize' what she characterized as egregious actions by the administration. The sharp divide reflects the broader national split over the Trump administration's first year in office.

stories.headline: Two Tren de Aragua members sentenced to 20 years in federal prison for $4 million Denver jewelry store robbery
stories.summary:
Two members of the Venezuelan prison gang Tren de Aragua were sentenced to 20 years in federal prison after pleading guilty to robbing a Denver jewelry store at gunpoint in June 2024. Jean Torres-Ramon, 22, and Newman Castillo Delgado, 23, were convicted on charges of robbery and brandishing a firearm in furtherance of a crime of violence, the U.S. Attorney's Office for the District of Colorado announced. Both men must also pay $3.9 million in court-ordered restitution.

The robbery targeted Joyeria El Ruby at 5108 W. 38th Ave. in Denver's West Highland neighborhood. Prosecutors say a group of at least seven suspects entered the store, aimed guns at employees, beat workers with weapons and made off with nearly $4 million in gold and jewelry. A separate federal indictment alleges the robbery was sanctioned by Tren de Aragua leadership to enrich the gang.

Five other defendants — Oswaldo Lozada-Solis, Jesus Daniel Lara Del Toro, Derek Alexander Dun-Vargas, Briley Ballera-Farias and Edwuimar Nazareth Colina-Romero — still have open federal cases, according to court records. Torres-Ramon received a sentence of 235 months, while Castillo Delgado received the full 20-year term.

stories.headline: Denver man, 20, arrested on murder charge in fatal Valentine's Day house party shooting
stories.summary:
Denver police arrested Yeanbraiker Yriarte-Valera, 20, in connection with a fatal shooting that occurred during a house party in the early hours of February 14 in the 1500 block of West Maple Avenue. Yriarte-Valera faces a first-degree murder charge after investigators determined he shot and killed an adult man who was found suffering from multiple gunshot wounds on the building's front porch. A woman at the party was also hospitalized with a gunshot wound to her ankle.

According to a probable cause affidavit, police found evidence of a large gathering inside the home, including suspected alcohol containers, oversized speakers and a light display in the basement. Officers recovered 25 spent 9mm shell casings in the street and 10 more inside the home and on its staircase, along with multiple bullet defects on exterior structures.

Witness interviews, conducted with the assistance of a Spanish-speaking officer, established that a street fight involving multiple men and one woman preceded the shooting. The altercation reportedly involved a woman's current and former boyfriends and had a history of domestic violence between the parties. The investigation is ongoing.

stories.headline: Measles case confirmed at Denver airport and Littleton church; free vaccinations offered
stories.summary:
Colorado public health officials confirmed Tuesday that an infectious measles patient traveled through Denver International Airport and attended a church service in Littleton over the weekend, potentially exposing dozens of people. The traveler arrived at DIA on Saturday morning on Frontier flight 1279 from Miami and attended a service and reception at St. Mary's Catholic Church in Littleton on Sunday. Anyone present at gate A83 between 9:15 a.m. and noon Saturday, or at the church between 9:30 a.m. and 2 p.m. Sunday, may have been exposed.

The traveler departed DIA Sunday night on Frontier flight 1280 back to Miami, and anyone at the airport between 9:30 p.m. Sunday and 1:30 a.m. Monday was also potentially exposed. The Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment noted that because the individual is fully vaccinated, transmission risk is lower but not eliminated.

Free measles vaccination clinics were offered beginning Wednesday at the Arapahoe County Public Health Department in Englewood and at St. Mary's Catholic Church. Officials noted that Colorado recorded 36 measles cases in 2025, the highest single-year total since at least 1996, with most involving unvaccinated individuals. One measles case had already been reported in Arapahoe County in 2026 prior to this exposure.

stories.headline: DIA broadens Peña Boulevard study to include mass transit options after advocacy pressure
stories.summary:
Denver International Airport officials have expanded the scope of their federally mandated review of Peña Boulevard congestion to include mass transit alternatives, reversing an earlier approach that focused primarily on widening the roadway. Rail expansion along the RTD A Line and dedicated bus lanes will now be evaluated as core alternatives alongside road-widening options as part of the National Environmental Policy Act review process. The shift was confirmed by DIA project manager Danielle Yearsley.

The change follows sustained pressure from environmental and transit advocacy groups including GreenLatinos and Greater Denver Transit, who argued that highway expansion would worsen air quality and only temporarily ease traffic before inducing new demand. Average drive times along the 11-mile corridor from Interstate 70 to the terminal have tripled from eight minutes to 24 minutes in recent years, with peak-period jams sometimes exceeding 30 minutes.

RTD currently runs A Line trains to DIA every 15 minutes during peak hours at $10 per one-way trip, with frequency dropping to every 30 minutes in off-peak hours. The full project is expected to cost hundreds of millions of dollars, and funding remains uncertain. The FAA must ultimately approve DIA's final selected alternative.

stories.headline: Denver Pavilions owner hires Urban Land Institute to chart future of downtown mall
stories.summary:
The Denver Downtown Development Authority has engaged the Urban Land Institute to produce a strategic recommendations report on the future of Denver Pavilions and two adjacent parking lots. An advisory panel of five or six outside real estate experts will visit Denver from April 13-17 and deliver a report roughly a month later. The DDDA is paying ULI $100,000 for the service.

The DDDA purchased the 350,000-square-foot mall at 500 16th St. in December for $37 million and acquired two nearby parking lots from Brookfield Properties for $23 million, stepping in after the previous owner, Gart Properties, defaulted on the property's loan. The mall is currently about 60 percent leased, generating $5.3 million annually in rent, while parking operations add another $3.2 million per year. Gart Properties continues to manage the property for a fee of $379,000.

The DDDA has issued two separate requests for proposals seeking retail leasing consultants and architecture or urban planning firms to develop a redevelopment vision ahead of the ULI visit. Chief Projects Officer Bill Mosher said the city's preference is to transfer ownership to a private developer as quickly as possible, maintaining flexibility until a clear vision is established. Submissions for both RFPs are due March 13.

stories.headline: Broncos intensify search for linebacker at NFL Combine as Sean Payton calls position a 'must' need
stories.summary:
The Denver Broncos used the opening days of the NFL Combine in Indianapolis to conduct formal meetings with multiple inside linebacker prospects, with head coach Sean Payton publicly declaring that addressing the position is a 'must' this offseason. Key starters Alex Singleton and Justin Strnad are both set to hit free agency, leaving a significant gap at the center of Denver's defense. General Manager George Paton acknowledged the challenge of finding a rookie linebacker capable of contributing immediately.

Georgia's CJ Allen and Texas Tech's Jacob Rodriguez emerged as leading candidates based on Denver's reported interest. Allen, who recorded 88 tackles in 2025 and draws praise for his leadership and sideline-to-sideline range, told reporters he would have 'no doubt' telling any team he is ready to start immediately. Rodriguez, the 2025 Bronko Nagurski Trophy winner, said his formal meeting with Denver went 'really smooth' and expressed strong interest in joining the organization.

The Broncos hold the 30th overall pick in the first round of the 2026 NFL Draft. Multiple national analysts, including projections from ESPN, The Athletic and USA Today, have mocked Allen or other linebackers to Denver in the first round. Payton's defense was among the best in the NFL this past season, and team personnel are focused on sustaining that standard through the draft.
